items.title: Consent is the foundation of legitimate screen sharing
items.description: Screen sharing is only legitimate when the person at the shared machine has knowingly and freely agreed to share their screen for a specific session. The tool exists to help a willing user show their own screen, not to let a third party observe a machine covertly.
items.applicability: Every design decision, default, and UI string in a screen-sharing client and server.
items.example: Before any frame is captured the client requires the local user to click a clearly labelled Start Sharing button; sharing never begins automatically on launch.
items.limitation: Consent in software cannot by itself prove the human truly understood; clear wording and a visible client are still required, and legal consent requirements vary by jurisdiction and relationship.

items.title: The client window must always be visible to the local user
items.description: The sharing client must run as a visible window that the local user can see and interact with. It must not run hidden, minimised-to-nothing, as a tray-only background process, or as an invisible window. A user who is sharing must be able to see at a glance that the client is running.
items.applicability: Window and process design of the screen-sharing client application.
items.example: On start the client shows a normal titled window; there is no option to hide it, and closing the window stops sharing.
items.limitation: A visible window does not stop a user from deliberately covering it; visibility is necessary but the user remains responsible for their own machine.

items.title: No stealth, hidden-window, or tray-only operation
items.description: Stealth operation is the defining property of spyware. The client must never offer a hidden mode, a silent install, a window that paints nothing, or a tray-only existence with no main window. Any such capability turns a consent tool into a surveillance tool.
items.applicability: Feature scope and code review of the client; a forbidden-capability checklist.
items.example: A code review rejects any pull request that adds a /silent or /hidden command-line switch or a ShowWindow(SW_HIDE) call on the main window.
items.limitation: The absence of stealth features does not prevent a malicious operator from social-engineering a user into running it; honest UI reduces but cannot remove that risk.

items.title: No persistence, autostart, service install, or scheduled task
items.description: The tool must not install itself to run automatically. No registry Run keys, no Startup-folder shortcuts, no Windows Service registration, no scheduled task, no auto-relaunch. It runs only when the user starts it and stops completely when the user closes it.
items.applicability: Installer/packaging behaviour and client startup code.
items.example: The packaged client is a plain folder with an EXE; running it starts a session, closing it ends the session, and nothing remains running or scheduled afterwards.
items.limitation: A user may still create their own shortcut; the rule governs what the product does automatically, not what a user manually chooses to set up.

items.title: No UAC bypass or privilege escalation
items.description: The client runs with the normal privileges of the user who started it. It must not attempt to bypass User Account Control, request silent elevation, exploit auto-elevating COM interfaces, or install drivers. Capturing the primary screen for the logged-in user needs no elevation.
items.applicability: Process manifest, capture implementation, and security review.
items.example: The application manifest requests asInvoker, not requireAdministrator, and the capture path uses ordinary user-session GDI calls.
items.limitation: Some enterprise lock-down configurations may still block even unprivileged capture; that is an environment constraint, not a reason to escalate.

items.title: No keyboard capture or keylogging
items.description: The tool must never install keyboard hooks, read global keystrokes, or log what the user types. SetWindowsHookEx with WH_KEYBOARD/WH_KEYBOARD_LL, GetAsyncKeyState polling loops, and raw-input keystroke capture are forbidden. The product shares pixels of the screen, nothing more.
items.applicability: Forbidden-API list enforced by code review and an automated source scan.
items.example: An automated test greps the product source for SetWindowsHookEx, WH_KEYBOARD_LL, and GetAsyncKeyState and fails the build if any appears.
items.limitation: A source scan cannot detect keystroke capture hidden in a binary third-party dependency; dependencies must therefore be minimal and trusted.

items.title: No clipboard capture or exfiltration
items.description: The tool must not read, monitor, or transmit the Windows clipboard. Clipboard contents frequently include passwords and personal data; a screen-share tool has no legitimate need to access them.
items.applicability: Forbidden-API list and source scan of the client and server.
items.example: The source scan flags OpenClipboard, GetClipboardData, and AddClipboardFormatListener as forbidden in the product code.
items.limitation: Screen pixels may still incidentally show clipboard content a user pastes on screen; that is inherent to sharing a screen and is covered by the visible-sharing warning, not by clipboard access.

items.title: No file access, browsing, or exfiltration
items.description: The tool must not enumerate, read, upload, or transmit the user's files. It has no file browser, no directory listing, no upload channel. The only data leaving the machine is the screen image stream of the primary display.
items.applicability: Network and filesystem behaviour; security review and source scan.
items.example: The server protocol defines only frame, heartbeat, and disconnect messages; there is no file-transfer message type and no filesystem read outside the application's own folder.
items.limitation: The screen image can itself reveal a file that is open on screen; this is intrinsic to screen sharing and is disclosed by the sharing warning rather than prevented.

items.title: No webcam or microphone capture
items.description: The tool captures the screen only. It must never open the webcam or microphone, and must declare no audio/video capture device usage. Sharing a screen does not require seeing or hearing the user.
items.applicability: Device-access review and source scan of the client.
items.example: The source scan flags Media Foundation / DirectShow capture APIs and any microphone WASAPI capture as forbidden in the product code.
items.limitation: Audio that is playing on the machine could in principle be part of a screen experience, but this product deliberately excludes all audio to keep scope minimal and honest.

items.title: No remote control of the shared machine
items.description: The product is one-way: it shows the local user's screen to a viewer. The viewer must not be able to move the mouse, send keystrokes, run commands, or otherwise control the shared machine. There is no input-injection channel.
items.applicability: Protocol design and server capabilities; security review.
items.example: The protocol carries frames from client to viewer only; there is no reverse input message, and SendInput / mouse_event / keybd_event do not appear anywhere in the product source.
items.limitation: View-only still lets a viewer read sensitive on-screen data; the protection against that is informed consent plus the visible sharing warning, not control restriction alone.

items.title: Capture the primary screen only
items.description: The tool shares only the user's primary display. It must not enumerate and silently grab secondary monitors, individual hidden windows, or off-screen surfaces. Limiting capture to the primary screen keeps the shared surface predictable and easy for the user to reason about.
items.example: The capture path resolves the primary monitor bounds (the monitor containing the origin) and copies only that rectangle; additional monitors are ignored.
items.limitation: A user who drags sensitive content onto the primary screen will share it; primary-only limits surprise but does not curate content.

items.title: Every stream requires token authentication
items.description: A viewer must present a valid session token before any frame is sent. The token is generated per session, shared out-of-band with the intended viewer, and validated by the server on connect. No token, no frames.
items.example: On WebSocket connect the server reads the token from the handshake, compares it in constant time to the session token, and closes the connection immediately if it does not match before streaming anything.
items.limitation: A token shared carelessly (e.g. posted publicly) can be reused by anyone who obtains it; token secrecy and short session lifetime are the user's and operator's responsibility.

items.title: No unauthenticated endpoint streams frames
items.description: There must be no endpoint, debug route, or fallback path that serves screen frames without authentication. A health/status endpoint may exist but must never expose pixels. Every pixel-bearing path is gated by token validation.
items.applicability: Server route inventory and security review.
items.example: A health endpoint returns only status JSON; the frame WebSocket rejects any connection whose token check fails, and there is no /preview or /debug route that bypasses it.
items.limitation: Operators can still misconfigure a reverse proxy to expose internals; the application guarantees only its own routes, not the surrounding infrastructure.

items.title: The client must display server address, session ID, and status
items.description: While sharing, the client must continuously show: the server address it is connected to, the current session ID, the live sharing status (sharing / not sharing / connecting), a Disconnect control, and a plain-language warning that the primary screen is being shared.
items.example: The client window shows 'Connected to 203.0.113.10:8080 | Session: 7QF3-22 | Status: SHARING' with a red banner 'Your primary screen is being shared' and a large Disconnect button.
items.limitation: Showing this information does not guarantee the user reads it; clear, prominent presentation is required to give the user a fair chance to notice.

items.title: Show a persistent 'your primary screen is being shared' warning
items.description: Whenever frames are leaving the machine, a prominent, persistent warning must be visible stating that the primary screen is being shared. The warning must remain for the entire session and disappear only when sharing stops.
items.applicability: Active-sharing UI state of the client.
items.example: A red status banner is pinned to the top of the client window for the whole session and is removed the instant Disconnect is pressed or the connection drops.
items.limitation: If the user minimises or covers the client, the warning is not on screen; the warning informs an attentive user but cannot force attention.

items.title: A Disconnect control must stop sharing immediately
items.description: The client must offer an always-available Disconnect control that, when pressed, stops frame capture and closes the connection at once. Stopping must be instantaneous from the user's point of view and must not require confirmation dialogs that delay it.
items.example: Pressing Disconnect cancels the capture loop, sends a disconnect message, closes the socket, and updates status to 'Not sharing' within one frame interval.
items.limitation: In-flight frames already on the network may still arrive at the viewer momentarily after Disconnect; the capture stops immediately but the network cannot retract bytes already sent.

items.title: Legitimate screen sharing versus malware: the distinguishing tests
items.description: Tooling is legitimate screen-sharing software when it is visible, consented, non-persistent, view-only, single-purpose (screen pixels), authenticated, and honest about what it does. It crosses into malware when it hides, persists, captures input/clipboard/files/devices, allows remote control, or streams without consent. Each forbidden capability in this pack maps to a known spyware/RAT behaviour.
items.applicability: Design self-assessment and security review sign-off for any screen-sharing build.
items.example: A release checklist confirms: visible client (yes), autostart (no), keylogging (no), clipboard/file/device capture (no), remote control (no), token-gated streams (yes) — all required answers present, so the build is on the legitimate side of the line.
items.limitation: Passing these tests means the tool is not built to spy; it cannot prevent a determined operator from misusing a legitimate, consented session, which is a human-trust matter beyond software.

items.title: Consent must be specific, informed, and revocable
items.description: Valid consent is for a specific session and viewer, given after the user understands what is shared, and can be withdrawn at any moment. The product must make withdrawal as easy as granting it, and must not pre-tick, remember, or auto-renew consent across sessions.
items.applicability: Session lifecycle and consent UX of the client.
items.example: Each launch starts unshared; the user must explicitly start sharing for that session, and a single Disconnect withdraws consent with no 'remember my choice' option.
items.limitation: Software-level consent does not replace any legal or organisational authorisation that a given context may additionally require.
